archetypes.label: Marketplace (two-sided)
signals: Two kinds of users who see different things (buyers and sellers, owners and providers)
Money or bookings change hands between users, not just between user and you
Listings, profiles, reviews, and search are central
branch_questions: Do you want it live fast to test demand, or is this a polished long-term build?
Does money actually move between users (you pay out sellers), or are you just connecting people for now?

options.when: Get live fast — test demand, payments included
options.stack: Supabase (database + logins + file storage) + a simple web frontend + Stripe Connect for seller payouts
options.why: A marketplace has two sides who see different data; Supabase gives you a real database, logins, and per-role rules in one free project, so you skip building a backend from scratch. Stripe Connect is the non-obvious must — it pays money out to your sellers, which plain Stripe does not do.

alternative.when_instead: if the app is more chat/realtime-heavy than listings-heavy — but most marketplaces have relational data (listings linked to bookings linked to payouts) that fits Supabase's database better

options.stack: Next.js (full-stack) + Postgres (Supabase or Neon) + Stripe Connect + Resend for email
options.why: A full-stack Next.js app keeps your frontend and server logic in one place; Postgres handles the relational data a marketplace lives on; Stripe Connect still does payouts; Resend sends the booking and receipt emails.

alternative.stack: A dedicated backend (Django or NestJS) + Postgres
alternative.when_instead: only once you outgrow simple data rules or need heavy custom server logic — not on day one

options.when: Just connecting people, no payments yet
options.stack: Supabase (database + logins + storage) + a simple web frontend, with messaging — no payment integration yet
options.why: If money does not move between users yet, skip the payment complexity entirely; you just need profiles, listings, and a way for the two sides to message.

alternative.when_instead: when you turn on paid transactions between users

backend_checklist: A database with two user roles (e.g. buyer / seller) kept separate
Login for both sides
Payouts to sellers (Stripe Connect) — not just taking a payment
Search and filtering of listings
Notifications (email at minimum)

archetypes.label: SaaS / web app with logins
signals: People sign up, log in, and see their own data
You charge a recurring subscription, or plan to
A dashboard or workspace per user or per company
branch_questions: Is each customer an individual, or a company/team with several members?
Do you charge a recurring subscription, or is it free / one-time?

options.stack: Next.js + Clerk (logins) + Postgres (Supabase) + Stripe Billing
options.why: Clerk handles signup, login, and password resets so you do not build auth yourself; Stripe Billing handles recurring charges (which are different from a one-time payment); Postgres holds each user's data.

alternative.when_instead: if you'd rather keep auth and database under one roof and one less service — Clerk just gives a nicer login experience out of the box

options.stack: Next.js + Clerk Organizations + Postgres + Stripe Billing + a job runner (Inngest)
options.why: When customers are teams you need multi-tenancy — each company's data walled off and members invited per company. Clerk Organizations gives you that; a job runner handles background work like invite emails, webhooks, and scheduled reports.

alternative.when_instead: for a lighter setup if team features are simple — full multi-tenant infrastructure is worth it only when companies get large

options.when: Free or one-time, not a subscription
options.stack: Next.js + Clerk + Postgres (skip Stripe Billing; add Stripe Checkout only if you take one-time payments)
options.why: If there's no recurring charge you don't need a billing system; keep auth and data, and add a simple one-time checkout only when you actually sell something.

alternative.when_instead: when you introduce paid subscription plans

backend_checklist: Login, signup, and password reset
Each customer's data isolated from others (multi-tenancy if teams)
Subscription billing (recurring is not the same as one-time)
Background jobs for emails, webhooks, and scheduled work
An admin view to see and manage your users

archetypes.label: Content site / blog / landing page
signals: Mostly pages people read — articles, marketing, a blog, a landing page
You (not your users) create the content
SEO and speed matter; little or no login
branch_questions: Do you want to edit content yourself without touching code, or are you fine editing files?
Is it a few fixed pages, or lots of regularly-updated articles?

options.stack: Astro (or Next.js static) + a headless CMS (Sanity)
options.why: Astro builds fast, SEO-friendly pages instead of a heavy app you don't need. A headless CMS like Sanity lets you edit content in a friendly dashboard without code. There is no app-database here because there is no user data — that's the right call, not a missing piece.

alternative.when_instead: if you want the most familiar editing experience and a huge plugin ecosystem, and don't mind a heavier, slower site

options.stack: Framer or Webflow
options.why: If you want to design and publish visually with zero code, Framer or Webflow get a polished site live in hours. Best when you don't need custom logic.

alternative.when_instead: for one landing page — even simpler and cheaper

options.stack: Astro + Tailwind on free hosting (Vercel or Netlify)
options.why: A handful of pages don't need a CMS at all — write them once and deploy for free.

alternative.when_instead: if you'd rather not touch code even for a few pages

backend_checklist: Usually NONE — don't add a database to a brochure site
A CMS only if you'll edit content often
A contact form via a form service (e.g. Formspree), not a custom backend
Good SEO setup (meta tags, sitemap)
A fast, free host (Vercel / Netlify)

archetypes.label: Online store / e-commerce
signals: You sell physical or digital products
Cart, checkout, inventory, orders, and shipping are involved
Customers pay you directly for goods
branch_questions: Do you want to sell quickly with the least building, or do you need a fully custom storefront?
Physical products (shipping, inventory) or digital downloads only?

options.stack: Shopify
options.why: For selling products, Shopify already solves cart, checkout, inventory, tax, shipping, and payment compliance — building those yourself is months of work and real legal risk. Being honest: this one is not a free build (monthly plan plus payment fees), but it is the fastest safe path.

alternative.stack: Stripe Payment Links + a simple site
alternative.when_instead: if you sell only a handful of items — you can skip Shopify's subscription and drop in payment links for free

options.stack: Next.js + Shopify headless (Hydrogen) or open-source Medusa + Stripe
options.why: If you need a unique look or custom flow, keep a real commerce engine (Shopify headless or Medusa) doing the hard parts and build your own frontend on top — you don't hand-roll checkout.

alternative.when_instead: if you're already on WordPress and want commerce as a plugin

options.stack: Gumroad or Lemon Squeezy
options.why: For digital goods (ebooks, courses, downloads) Gumroad or Lemon Squeezy handle payment, delivery, and even sales tax with no store to build.

alternative.when_instead: if you want full control and lower fees and can accept more setup

backend_checklist: Product catalog and inventory
Cart and secure checkout — don't hand-build this
Payments, tax, and refunds
Orders and fulfillment / shipping
Customer accounts (optional if you allow guest checkout)

archetypes.label: Mobile app
signals: It runs on phones — an app people download from the App Store or Play Store
Push notifications, camera, location, or offline use matter
It is not primarily a website
branch_questions: Which devices — iPhone/iPad only, or Android too?
Does it need heavy phone features (AR, Bluetooth, games, on-device AI), or is it mostly screens plus data?

options.stack: Swift / SwiftUI (native) + Supabase or Firebase for the backend
options.why: If you're Apple-only and need top performance or deep device features (AR, advanced camera), native Swift is the best tool — full access to everything Apple offers. Xcode is free to build with; publishing costs 99 USD per year.

alternative.when_instead: if you might add Android later but still want near-native performance

options.stack: Expo (React Native) + Supabase or Firebase
options.why: One codebase ships to both stores, and if you or anyone knows web/JavaScript the skills carry straight over. Supabase or Firebase give you data, login, and push notifications free to start.

alternative.when_instead: if you want especially smooth, custom animated UI — Flutter excels at polished visuals

options.stack: Flutter + Supabase or Firebase
options.why: Flutter renders beautiful, fast UI from one codebase across both stores — the strongest choice when design polish is the point.

alternative.when_instead: if reusing existing web/JavaScript skills matters more than UI flourish

backend_checklist: Where your data lives (Supabase / Firebase)
Login
Push notifications (real work, plus app-store review)
Offline / sync if used away from signal
App Store and Play Store review time — budget for it

archetypes.label: Social / community app
signals: Users post, follow each other, and see a feed
Profiles, likes, comments, and direct messages
Content from many users shown to many users
branch_questions: Is the heart of it a feed/timeline, or more like group chat/messaging?
How much media (photos, video) will users upload?

options.stack: Supabase + a managed feed API (Stream) + Next.js or Expo
options.why: Feeds look simple but are genuinely hard — ranking, fanning a post out to all followers, and scale. A managed feed service (Stream) does that for you; Supabase holds profiles and auth. Plan media storage and moderation from day one.

alternative.stack: Build the feed yourself on Postgres
alternative.when_instead: only for a small, simple feed where you control the scale — graduate to a feed service when it grows

options.stack: Supabase + Stream Chat (or Firebase) + your frontend
options.why: Real-time chat — delivery, typing indicators, presence — is a solved problem; Stream Chat or Firebase realtime save you months versus building messaging from scratch.

alternative.when_instead: for lighter chat needs where you want everything in one platform

options.stack: Add Cloudinary or S3 + a CDN to whichever option above fits
options.why: Heavy media needs proper storage plus a CDN so it loads fast and doesn't blow up your bill; Cloudinary also resizes and optimizes images and video for you.

alternative.when_instead: for modest media volumes — simplest if you're already on Supabase

backend_checklist: Profiles and follow/friend relationships
The feed or chat engine (consider a managed service)
Media storage plus a CDN
Notifications (push and in-app)
Moderation / reporting — needed sooner than you think

archetypes.label: Internal tool / admin dashboard
signals: A tool for your own team, not the public
Mostly viewing and editing data in tables and forms (CRUD)
Connects to a database or APIs you already have
branch_questions: Is this a quick admin/ops tool over existing data, or a real product that will grow?
Does it mostly read and edit one database, or stitch together several services?

options.stack: Retool (or Appsmith / Budibase)
options.why: For an internal CRUD tool, low-code builders like Retool connect to your database and generate tables, forms, and buttons in hours. Hand-building auth, tables, and forms wastes weeks when the users are your own team.

alternative.when_instead: if you want to self-host for free or avoid per-user pricing as the team grows

options.when: Will grow into a real product
options.stack: Next.js + shadcn/ui + Postgres (or the Refine framework)
options.why: If it will outgrow low-code, a real codebase with a component kit (shadcn) gives you a clean admin UI you fully control; Refine is built specifically for data-heavy internal apps.

alternative.when_instead: until you hit a real limit — premature custom-building is the classic mistake here

backend_checklist: Connection to your existing database / APIs
Login plus who-can-do-what (roles)
Create, read, update, delete on your data
An audit trail of who changed what (often needed)
Kept private — not exposed to the public internet

archetypes.label: AI / LLM app (chatbot, generator)
signals: The core feature is AI — a chatbot, a generator, a smart assistant
It calls a language model and shows the result
It may answer questions over your own documents (RAG)
branch_questions: Is it straight chat/generation, or does it answer over your own documents/data (RAG)?
Will it run quick replies, or long jobs (big documents, batch generation)?

options.stack: Next.js + Vercel AI SDK + Claude API (start with the cheaper Haiku model) + Clerk for login
options.why: The Vercel AI SDK handles streaming responses (the typing effect) out of the box; Claude is the model doing the thinking. Start on a smaller, cheaper model (Haiku) and move up only if quality needs it — this keeps your bill under control.

alternative.when_instead: if your AI logic gets complex (multi-step agents, tool use) and you'd rather orchestrate it in Python

options.stack: Next.js + Vercel AI SDK + Claude API + Supabase pgvector for document storage
options.why: To answer over your documents you retrieve the relevant chunks and feed them to the model — that needs a vector store. Supabase pgvector keeps it in the database you already have, so no extra service.

alternative.stack: Pinecone or a dedicated vector database
alternative.when_instead: at large scale where a purpose-built vector database outperforms pgvector

options.stack: Add a job queue (Inngest or Trigger.dev) + a Python (FastAPI) backend for orchestration
options.why: Big documents or batch generation can't finish inside a single web request — a queue runs them in the background and reports progress, so the app stays responsive and you don't hit timeouts.

alternative.when_instead: if jobs are short enough to finish within request limits — add the queue only when they aren't

backend_checklist: The model API (Claude) — and which model, trading cost against quality
Streaming responses so it feels fast
Usage metering and cost limits (the bill is easy to forget)
A vector store if answering over your documents (RAG)
Background jobs for anything long-running
